code: tol.minus9 <- function(x, y, tol) {   idx1 <- is.na(x) & !is.na(y) & y == -9   idx2 <- tol.num.absolute(x, y, tol) # find other absolute differences   return(!idx1 & idx2) }  summary(comparedf(mockstudy, mockstudy2, by = "case",                 tol.vars = c("._ ", "case"), # dots=underscores=spaces, ignore case                 int.as.num = TRUE,           # compare integers and numerics                 tol.num.val = 10,            # allow absolute differences <= 10                 tol.factor = "labels",       # match only factor labels                 factor.as.char = TRUE,       # compare factors and characters                 tol.char = "case",           # ignore case in character vectors                 tol.num = tol.minus9         # ignore NA -> -9 changes ))

title: The freqlist function
text: Instead passing pre-computed table freqlist(), can instead pass formula, turn passed xtabs() function. Additional freqlist() arguments passed ... freqlist() table method. Note freqlist() sets addNA=TRUE argument default: One can also set NAs explicit value using includeNA(). fact, since xtabs() allows left-hand-side weights, freqlist()! can also specify multiple weights:
code: summary(freqlist(~ arm + sex + mdquality.s, data = mockstudy)) summary(freqlist(~ arm + sex + includeNA(mdquality.s, "Missing"), data = mockstudy)) mockstudy$weights <- c(10000, rep(1, nrow(mockstudy) - 1)) summary(freqlist(weights ~ arm + sex + addNA(mdquality.s), data = mockstudy)) mockstudy$weights2 <- c(rep(1, nrow(mockstudy) - 1), 10000) summary(freqlist(list(weights, weights2) ~ arm + sex + addNA(mdquality.s), data = mockstudy))

what: Rounding percentage digits or changing variable names for printing
title: The freqlist function
text: digits.pct= argument takes single numeric value controls number digits percentages output. digits.count= argument takes similar argument controls number digits count columns. labelTranslations= argument named character vector list. options applied following example.
code: example2 <- freqlist(tab.ex, labelTranslations = c(arm = "Treatment Arm", sex = "Gender", mdquality.s = "LASA QOL"),                       digits.pct = 1, digits.count = 1) summary(example2)

what: Including combinations with frequencies of zero
title: The freqlist function
text: sparse= argument takes single logical value input. default option FALSE. set TRUE, sparse option include combinations frequencies zero list results. initial table levels, create second table use example.
code: summary(freqlist(~ race + sex + arm, data = mockstudy, sparse = TRUE, digits.pct=1))

title: The freqlist function
text: various na.options= allow include exclude data missing values one factor levels counts percentages, well show missing data exclude cumulative counts percentages. default option include combinations missing values.
code: summary(freqlist(tab.ex, na.options="include")) summary(freqlist(tab.ex, na.options="showexclude")) summary(freqlist(tab.ex, na.options="remove"))

what: Frequency counts and percentages subset by factor levels
title: The freqlist function
text: strata= argument internally subsets data specified factor prior calculating cumulative counts percentages. default, used subset print separate table. Using single = TRUE option printing collapse subsetted result single table.
code: example3 <- freqlist(tab.ex, strata = c("arm","sex")) summary(example3) #using the single = TRUE argument will collapse results into a single table for printing summary(example3, single = TRUE)

what: Using xtable() to format and print freqlist() results
title: The freqlist function
text: Fair warning: xtable() kind steep learning curve. examples given without explanation, advanced users. xtable formatted output freqlist data frame
code: require(xtable) Loading required package: xtable # set up custom function for xtable text italic <- function(x) paste0('', x, '') xftbl <- xtable(as.data.frame(summary(example1)), caption = "xtable formatted output of freqlist data frame", align="|r|r|r|r|c|c|c|r|") # change the column names names(xftbl)[1:3] <- c("Arm", "Gender", "LASA QOL") print(xftbl, sanitize.colnames.function = italic, include.rownames = FALSE, type = "html", comment = FALSE)

previous_headings: Appendix: Notes regarding table options in R
title: The freqlist function
text: several widely used options basic tables R. table() function base R probably common; default excludes NA values. can change NA handling base::table() using useNA= exclude= arguments. xtabs() similar table(), uses formula-based syntax. However, NAs must explicitly added factor using addNA() function using argument addNA = TRUE. Since formula method freqlist() uses xtabs(), NAs treated way. includeNA() can also helpful setting explicit NA values.
code: # base table default removes NAs tab.d1 <- base::table(mockstudy[, c("arm", "sex", "mdquality.s")], useNA="ifany") tab.d1 , , mdquality.s = 0             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL      29     12   F: FOLFOX   31     21   G: IROX     17     14  , , mdquality.s = 1             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL     214    118   F: FOLFOX  285    198   G: IROX    187    121  , , mdquality.s = NA             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL      34     21   F: FOLFOX   95     61   G: IROX     24     17 # without specifying addNA tab.d2 <- xtabs(formula = ~ arm + sex + mdquality.s, data = mockstudy) tab.d2 , , mdquality.s = 0             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL      29     12   F: FOLFOX   31     21   G: IROX     17     14  , , mdquality.s = 1             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL     214    118   F: FOLFOX  285    198   G: IROX    187    121 # now with addNA tab.d3 <- xtabs(~ arm + sex + addNA(mdquality.s), data = mockstudy) tab.d3 , , addNA(mdquality.s) = 0             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL      29     12   F: FOLFOX   31     21   G: IROX     17     14  , , addNA(mdquality.s) = 1             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL     214    118   F: FOLFOX  285    198   G: IROX    187    121  , , addNA(mdquality.s) = NA             sex arm         Male Female   A: IFL      34     21   F: FOLFOX   95     61   G: IROX     24     17

title: A Few Notes on Labels
text: summary() method tableby(), modelsum(), freqlist() objects contains labelTranslations = argument specify labels function call. Note freqlist() function matches labels order, whereas two match labels name. labels can input list character vector.
code: summary(freqlist(tab.ex),         labelTranslations = c(arm = "Treatment Arm", sex = "Gender", mdquality.s = "LASA QOL")) summary(tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data = mockstudy),         labelTranslations = c(sex = "SEX", age = "Age, yrs")) summary(modelsum(bmi ~ age, adjust = ~sex, data = mockstudy),         labelTranslations = list(sexFemale = "Female", age = "Age, yrs"))

title: A Few Notes on Labels
text: Another option add labels created object. , can use form labels(x) <- value use pipe-able version, set_labels().
code: # the non-pipe version; somewhat clunky tmp <- freqlist(tab.ex) labels(tmp) <- c(arm = "Treatment Arm", sex = "Gender", mdquality.s = "LASA QOL") summary(tmp) # piped--much cleaner mockstudy %>%    tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data = .) %>%    set_labels(c(sex = "SEX", age = "Age, yrs")) %>%    summary() mockstudy %>%    modelsum(bmi ~ age, adjust = ~ sex, data = .) %>%    set_labels(list(sexFemale = "Female", age = "Age, yrs")) %>%    summary()

title: A Few Notes on Labels
text: tableby() modelsum() also allow label attributes data. Note default attributes usually get dropped upon subsetting, tableby() modelsum() use keep.labels() function retain . [1] “integer” [1] “keep_labels” “integer” undo , simply loosen.labels(): [1] “integer” can set attributes one time two ways: …: can pipe , . extract labels data.frame, simply use labels() function:
code: mockstudy.lab <- keep.labels(mockstudy) class(mockstudy$age) class(mockstudy.lab$age) class(loosen.labels(mockstudy.lab)$age) attr(mockstudy.lab$sex, "label") <- "Sex" labels(mockstudy.lab$age) <- "Age, yrs" labels(mockstudy.lab) <- list(sex = "Sex", age = "Age, yrs") summary(tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data = mockstudy.lab)) mockstudy %>%    set_labels(list(sex = "SEX", age = "Age, yrs")) %>%    modelsum(bmi ~ age, adjust = ~ sex, data = .) %>%    summary() labels(mockstudy.lab) ## $case ## NULL ##  ## $age ## [1] "Age, yrs" ##  ## $arm ## [1] "Treatment Arm" ##  ## $sex ## [1] "Sex" ##  ## $race ## [1] "Race" ##  ## $fu.time ## NULL ##  ## $fu.stat ## NULL ##  ## $ps ## NULL ##  ## $hgb ## NULL ##  ## $bmi ## [1] "Body Mass Index (kg/m^2)" ##  ## $alk.phos ## NULL ##  ## $ast ## NULL ##  ## $mdquality.s ## NULL ##  ## $age.ord ## NULL

title: A Few Notes on Labels
text: tableby() modelsum() support wrapping long labels. Consider width= argument print() function:
code: mockstudy %>%    set_labels(list(age = "This is a really long label for the arm variable")) %>%    tableby(sex ~ age, data = .) %>%    summary() %>%    print(width = 20)

title: The modelsum function
text: often asked summarize model results multiple fits nice table. endpoint might different types (e.g., survival, case/control, continuous) may several independent variables want examine univariately adjusted certain variables age sex. Locally Mayo, SAS macros %modelsum, %glmuniv, %logisuni written create summary tables. increasing interest R, developed function modelsum create similar tables within R environment. developing modelsum function, goal bring best features macros R function. However, task simply duplicate functionality, rather make use R’s strengths (modeling, method dispersion, flexibility function definition output format) make tool fits needs R users. Additionally, results needed fit within general reproducible research framework tables displayed within R markdown report. report provides step--step directions using functions associated modelsum. functions presented available within arsenal package. assumption made users somewhat familiar R markdown documents. new topic, good initial resource available rmarkdown.rstudio.com.

title: The modelsum function

title: The modelsum function

title: The modelsum function
text: Poisson regression useful predicting outcome variable representing counts. can also useful looking survival data. Cox models Poisson models closely related survival data can summarized using Poisson regression. overdispersion (see residual deviance much larger degrees freedom), may want use quasipoisson() instead poisson(). diagnostics need done outside modelsum.

text: second example uses survival endpoint available mockstudy dataset. close relationship survival Poisson models, often easier fit model using Poisson regression, especially want present absolute risk.

what: 5. Summarize multiple variables without typing them out
title: The modelsum function
text: Often one wants summarize number variables. Instead typing hand individual variable, alternative approach create formula using paste command collapse="+" option. [1] “ps+hgb+bmi+alk.phos+ast” mdquality.s ~ ps + hgb + bmi + alk.phos + ast steps can also done using formulize function. mdquality.s ~ ps + hgb + bmi
code: > # create a vector specifying the variable names > myvars <- names(mockstudy) >  > # select the 8th through the 12th > # paste them together, separated by the + sign > RHS <- paste(myvars[8:12], collapse="+") > RHS >  > # create a formula using the as.formula function > as.formula(paste('mdquality.s ~ ', RHS)) >  > # use the formula in the modelsum function > summary(modelsum(as.formula(paste('mdquality.s ~', RHS)), family=binomial, data=mockstudy)) > ## The formulize function does the paste and as.formula steps > tmp <- formulize('mdquality.s',myvars[8:10]) > tmp >  > ## More complex formulas could also be written using formulize > tmp2 <- formulize('mdquality.s',c('ps','hgb','sqrt(bmi)')) >  > ## use the formula in the modelsum function > summary(modelsum(tmp, data=mockstudy, family=binomial))

what: 6. Subset the dataset used in the analysis
title: The modelsum function
text: two ways get result (limit analysis subjects age>50 F: FOLFOX treatment group). first approach uses subset function applied dataset mockstudy. example also selects subset variables. modelsum function applied subsetted data. second approach analysis uses subset argument within modelsum subset data.
code: > newdata <- subset(mockstudy, subset=age>50 & arm=='F: FOLFOX', select = c(age,sex, bmi:alk.phos)) > dim(mockstudy) [1] 1499   14 > table(mockstudy$arm)     A: IFL F: FOLFOX   G: IROX        428       691       380  > dim(newdata) [1] 557   4 > names(newdata) [1] "age"      "sex"      "bmi"      "alk.phos" > summary(modelsum(alk.phos ~ ., data=newdata)) > summary(modelsum(log(alk.phos) ~ sex + ps + bmi, subset=age>50 & arm=="F: FOLFOX", data=mockstudy)) > summary(modelsum(alk.phos ~ ps + bmi, adjust=~sex, subset = age>50 & bmi<24, data=mockstudy)) > summary(modelsum(alk.phos ~ ps + bmi, adjust=~sex, subset=1:30, data=mockstudy))

code: > ## look at how many missing values there are for each variable > apply(is.na(mockstudy),2,sum)        case         age         arm         sex        race     fu.time            0           0           0           0           7           0      fu.stat          ps         hgb         bmi    alk.phos         ast            0         266         266          33         266         266  mdquality.s     age.ord          252           0 > ## Show how many subjects have each variable (non-missing) > summary(modelsum(bmi ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy, +                 control=modelsum.control(gaussian.stats=c("N","estimate")))) >  > ## Always list the number of missing values > summary(modelsum(bmi ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy, +                 control=modelsum.control(gaussian.stats=c("Nmiss2","estimate")))) >  > ## Only show the missing values if there are some (default) > summary(modelsum(bmi ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,  +                 control=modelsum.control(gaussian.stats=c("Nmiss","estimate")))) >  > ## Don't show N at all > summary(modelsum(bmi ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,  +                 control=modelsum.control(gaussian.stats=c("estimate"))))

Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    2.923   3.225   3.548   3.502   3.844   4.045   $`F: FOLFOX`    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu. title: The paired function
text: first step using paired() function load arsenal package. can’t use mockstudy need dataset paired observations, ’ll create dataset. create simple table stratified time point, use formula= statement specify variables want summarized id= argument specify paired observations. third column shows difference time point 1 time point 2. categorical variables, reports percent observations time point 1 changed time point 2.
code: library(arsenal) dat <- data.frame(   tp = paste0("Time Point ", c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2)),   id = c(1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6),   Cat = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "B", "B", "A", NA, "B"),   Fac = factor(c("A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A")),   Num = c(1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 3, 4, 0, NA),   Ord = ordered(c("I", "II", "II", "III", "III", "III", "I", "III", "II", "I")),   Lgl = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE),   Dat = as.Date("2018-05-01") + c(1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 3, 4),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) p <- paired(tp ~ Cat + Fac + Num + Ord + Lgl + Dat, data = dat, id = id, signed.rank.exact = FALSE) summary(p)

title: The paired function
text: Note default, observations timepoints removed. easily changed using na.action = na.paired("<arg>") argument. example: details, see help page na.paired().

title: The tableby function
text: example, age shown label (Age Years), sex listed “” lower case letters. data created SAS SAS dataset, age label sex . label stored attribute within R. want add labels variables, couple options. First, add labels variables dataset. can also use built-data.frame method labels<-: Another option add labels created table Alternatively, can check variable labels manipulate function called labels, works tableby object.
code: ## Look at one variable's label attr(mockstudy$age,'label') ## [1] "Age in Years" ## See all the variables with a label unlist(lapply(mockstudy,'attr','label')) ##                        age                        arm                       race  ##             "Age in Years"            "Treatment Arm"                     "Race"  ##                        bmi  ## "Body Mass Index (kg/m^2)" # Can also use labels(mockstudy) attr(mockstudy$sex,'label')  <- 'Gender'  tab1 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy) summary(tab1) labels(mockstudy)  <- c(age = 'Age, yrs', sex = "Gender")  tab1 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy) summary(tab1) mylabels <- list(sex = "SEX", age = "Age, yrs") summary(tab1, labelTranslations = mylabels) labels(tab1) ##             arm             sex             age  ## "Treatment Arm"        "Gender"      "Age, yrs" labels(tab1) <- c(arm="Treatment Assignment", age="Baseline Age (yrs)") labels(tab1) ##                    arm                    sex                    age  ## "Treatment Assignment"               "Gender"   "Baseline Age (yrs)" summary(tab1)

title: The tableby function
text: Currently default behavior summarize continuous variables : Number missing values, Mean (SD), 25th - 75th quantiles, Minimum-Maximum values ANOVA (t-test equal variances) p-value. categorical variables default show: Number missing values count (column percent) chi-square p-value. behavior can modified using tableby.control function. fact, can save standard settings use future tables. Note test=FALSE total=FALSE results total column p-value column printed. can also change settings directly tableby call.
code: mycontrols  <- tableby.control(test=FALSE, total=FALSE,                                numeric.test="kwt", cat.test="chisq",                                numeric.stats=c("N", "median", "q1q3"),                                cat.stats=c("countpct"),                                stats.labels=list(N='Count', median='Median', q1q3='Q1,Q3')) tab2 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy, control=mycontrols) summary(tab2) tab3 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy, test=FALSE, total=FALSE,                  numeric.stats=c("median","q1q3"), numeric.test="kwt") summary(tab3)

what: Change summary statistics within the formula
title: The tableby function
text: addition modifying summary options globally, possible modify test summary statistics specific variables within formula statement. example, kwt (Kruskal-Wallis rank-based) anova (asymptotic analysis variance) tests apply numeric variables, can use one variable “age”, another variable “bmi”, test variable “ast”. list options shown end vignette. tests function can quick check tests performed variable tableby. Summary statistics individual variable can also modified, must done secondary arguments test function. function names must strings functions already written tableby, built-R functions like mean range, user-defined functions. can also passed stats= argument.
code: tab.test <- tableby(arm ~ kwt(age) + anova(bmi) + notest(ast), data=mockstudy) tests(tab.test) ##           Group Variable   p.value                       Method ## 1 Treatment Arm      age 0.6390614 Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test ## 2 Treatment Arm      bmi 0.8916552           Linear Model ANOVA ## 3 Treatment Arm      ast        NA                      No test summary(tab.test) tab.test <- tableby(arm ~ kwt(ast, "Nmiss2","median") + anova(age, "N","mean") +                     notest(bmi, "Nmiss","median"), data=mockstudy) summary(tab.test) tab.test <- tableby(arm ~ kwt(ast, stats = c("Nmiss2", "median")) + anova(age, stats = c("N","mean")) +                     notest(bmi, stats = c("Nmiss","median")), data=mockstudy) summary(tab.test)

what: Controlling Options for Categorical Tests (Chisq and Fisher’s)
title: The tableby function
text: formal tests categorical variables levels variable, chisq fe, options simulate p-values. show turn simulations 500 replicates Fisher’s test (fe). 1 Treatment Arm sex 0.2195609 2 Treatment Arm race 0.3093812 Method 1 Fisher’s Exact Test Count Data simulated p-valuebased 500 replicates) 2 Fisher’s Exact Test Count Data simulated p-valuebased 500 replicates) chi-square test 2x2 tables applies Yates’ continuity correction default, provide option turn correction. show results without correction applied treatment arm sex, use subset ignore one three treatment arms. 1 Treatment Arm sex 0.1666280 Pearson’s Chi-squared test 2 Treatment Arm race 0.8108543 Pearson’s Chi-squared test 1 Treatment Arm sex 0.1666280 Pearson’s Chi-squared test 2 Treatment Arm race 0.8108543 Pearson’s Chi-squared test
code: set.seed(100) tab.catsim <- tableby(arm ~ sex + race, cat.test="fe", simulate.p.value=TRUE, B=500, data=mockstudy) tests(tab.catsim) Group Variable   p.value cat.correct <- tableby(arm ~ sex + race, cat.test="chisq", subset = !grepl("^F", arm), data=mockstudy) tests(cat.correct) Group Variable   p.value                     Method cat.nocorrect <- tableby(arm ~ sex + race, cat.test="chisq", subset = !grepl("^F", arm),      chisq.correct=FALSE, data=mockstudy) tests(cat.nocorrect) Group Variable   p.value                     Method

title: The tableby function
text: Date variables default summarized number missing values, median, range. example purposes ’ve created random date. Missing values introduced impossible February dates.
code: set.seed(100) N <- nrow(mockstudy) mockstudy$dtentry <- mdy.Date(month=sample(1:12,N,replace=T), day=sample(1:29,N,replace=T),                                year=sample(2005:2009,N,replace=T)) summary(tableby(sex ~ dtentry, data=mockstudy))

what: 6. Summarize multiple variables without typing them out
title: The tableby function
text: Often one wants summarize number variables. Instead typing hand individual variable, alternative approach create formula using paste command collapse="+" option. [1] “ps+hgb+bmi” arm ~ ps + hgb + bmi steps can also done using formulize function. arm ~ ps + hgb + bmi change summary statistics statistical tests en masse, consider using paste0() together formulize(): Kruskal-Wallis rank sum test Pearson’s Chi-squared test Linear Model ANOVA
code: ## create a vector specifying the variable names myvars <- names(mockstudy)  ## select the 8th through the last variables ## paste them together, separated by the + sign RHS <- paste(myvars[8:10], collapse="+") RHS ## create a formula using the as.formula function as.formula(paste('arm ~ ', RHS)) ## use the formula in the tableby function summary(tableby(as.formula(paste('arm ~', RHS)), data=mockstudy)) ## The formulize function does the paste and as.formula steps tmp <- formulize('arm',myvars[8:10]) tmp ## More complex formulas could also be written using formulize tmp2 <- formulize('arm',c('ps','hgb^2','bmi'))  ## use the formula in the tableby function summary(tableby(tmp, data=mockstudy)) varlist1 <- c('age','sex','hgb') varlist2 <- paste0("anova(", c('bmi','alk.phos','ast'), ", 'meansd')")  summary(tableby(formulize("arm", c(varlist1, varlist2)),                 data = mockstudy, numeric.test = "kwt"), pfootnote = TRUE)

what: 7. Subset the dataset used in the analysis
title: The tableby function
text: two ways get result (limit analysis subjects age>5 F: FOLFOX treatment group). first approach uses subset function applied dataset mockstudy. example also selects subset variables. tableby function applied subsetted data. second approach analysis uses subset argument within tableby subset data.
code: newdata <- subset(mockstudy, subset=age>50 & arm=='F: FOLFOX', select = c(sex,ps:bmi)) dim(mockstudy) ## [1] 1499   16 table(mockstudy$arm) ##  ##    A: IFL F: FOLFOX   G: IROX  ##       428       691       380 dim(newdata) ## [1] 557   4 names(newdata) ## [1] "sex" "ps"  "hgb" "bmi" summary(tableby(sex ~ ., data=newdata)) summary(tableby(sex ~ ps + hgb + bmi, subset=age>50 & arm=="F: FOLFOX", data=mockstudy))

what: 8. Create combinations of variables on the fly
title: The tableby function
code: ## create a variable combining the levels of mdquality.s and sex with(mockstudy, table(interaction(mdquality.s,sex))) ##  ##   0.Male   1.Male 0.Female 1.Female  ##       77      686       47      437 summary(tableby(arm ~ interaction(mdquality.s,sex), data=mockstudy)) ## create a new grouping variable with combined levels of arm and sex summary(tableby(interaction(mdquality.s, sex) ~  age + bmi, data=mockstudy, subset=arm=="F: FOLFOX"))

title: The tableby function
text: Certain transformations need surrounded () R knows treat variable transformation special model feature. transformation includes symbols / - + ^ * surround new variable (). labels variables aren’t exactly ’d like, can change modify fact. Instead typing long variable names, can modify specific labels position. Note changed mdquality.s factor, summarized though continuous variable. [1] “integer” Another option specify test summary statistics. fact, set variables coded 0/1 summarizing, change global option continuous variables use chi-square test show countpct.
code: trans <- tableby(arm ~ I(age/10) + log(bmi) + factor(mdquality.s, levels=0:1, labels=c('N','Y')),                  data=mockstudy) summary(trans) labels(trans) ##                                                           arm  ##                                               "Treatment Arm"  ##                                                     I(age/10)  ##                                                    "Age, yrs"  ##                                                      log(bmi)  ##                                    "Body Mass Index (kg/m^2)"  ##       factor(mdquality.s, levels = 0:1, labels = c("N", "Y"))  ## "factor(mdquality.s, levels = 0:1, labels = c(\"N\", \"Y\"))" labels(trans)[2:4] <- c('Age per 10 yrs', 'log(BMI)', 'MD Quality') labels(trans) ##                                                     arm  ##                                         "Treatment Arm"  ##                                               I(age/10)  ##                                        "Age per 10 yrs"  ##                                                log(bmi)  ##                                              "log(BMI)"  ## factor(mdquality.s, levels = 0:1, labels = c("N", "Y"))  ##                                            "MD Quality" summary(trans) class(mockstudy$mdquality.s) summary(tableby(arm~mdquality.s, data=mockstudy)) summary(tableby(arm ~ chisq(mdquality.s, "Nmiss","countpct"), data=mockstudy))

what: 10. Subsetting (change the ordering of the variables, delete a variable, sort by p-value, filter by p-value, show only certain by-levels)
title: The tableby function
text: tableby Object Function Call: tableby(formula = arm ~ sex + alk.phos + age, data = mockstudy) Variable(s): arm ~ sex
code: mytab <- tableby(arm ~ sex + alk.phos + age, data=mockstudy) mytab2 <- mytab[c('age','sex','alk.phos')] summary(mytab2) summary(mytab[c('age','sex')], digits = 2) summary(mytab[c(3,1)], digits = 3) summary(sort(mytab, decreasing = TRUE)) summary(mytab[mytab < 0.5]) head(mytab, 1) # can also use tail() summary(tableby(list(arm, sex) ~ sex + alk.phos + age, data=mockstudy)[, "sex"]) summary(tableby(list(arm, sex) ~ sex + alk.phos + age, data=mockstudy)[, list(sex = "Female", arm = c("F: FOLFOX", "Total"))])

title: The tableby function
text: possible combine two tableby objects print together. Overlapping -variables x-variables concatenated, (=TRUE) non-overlapping -variables tables printed separately. [1] “tableby” “arsenal_table” tables two different outcomes, consider =TRUE argument:
code: ## demographics tab1 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy,                 control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("Nmiss","meansd"), total=FALSE)) ## lab data tab2 <- tableby(arm ~ hgb + alk.phos, data=mockstudy,                 control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("Nmiss","median","q1q3"),                                         numeric.test="kwt", total=FALSE)) tab12 <- merge(tab1, tab2) class(tab12) summary(tab12) summary(merge(   tableby(sex ~ age, data = mockstudy),   tableby(arm ~ bmi, data = mockstudy),   all = TRUE ))

what: 12. Add a title to the table
title: The tableby function
text: creating pdf tables automatically numbered title appears table. Word HTML, titles appear un-numbered table. Demographics multiple left-hand sides, can pass vector list determine labels table: arm table sex table
code: t1 <- tableby(arm ~ sex + age, data=mockstudy) summary(t1, title='Demographics') summary(tableby(list(arm, sex) ~ age, data = mockstudy), title = c("arm table", "sex table"))

what: 13. Modify how missing values are displayed
title: The tableby function
text: Depending report writing following options: Show many subjects variable Show many subjects missing variable Show many subjects missing variable missing values Don’t indicate missing values One might also consider use includeNA() include NAs counts percents categorical variables.
code: ## look at how many missing values there are for each variable apply(is.na(mockstudy),2,sum) ##        case         age         arm         sex        race     fu.time     fu.stat          ps  ##           0           0           0           0           7           0           0         266  ##         hgb         bmi    alk.phos         ast mdquality.s     age.ord  age.ordnew     dtentry  ##         266          33         266         266         252           0           1           5 ## Show how many subjects have each variable (non-missing) summary(tableby(sex ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,                 control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("N","median"), total=FALSE))) ## Always list the number of missing values summary(tableby(sex ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,                 control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("Nmiss2","median"), total=FALSE))) ## Only show the missing values if there are some (default) summary(tableby(sex ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,                  control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("Nmiss","mean"),total=FALSE))) ## Don't show N at all summary(tableby(sex ~ ast + age, data=mockstudy,                  control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("mean"),total=FALSE))) mockstudy$ps.cat <- factor(mockstudy$ps) attr(mockstudy$ps.cat, "label") <- "ps" summary(tableby(sex ~ includeNA(ps.cat), data = mockstudy, cat.stats = "countpct"))

what: 14. Modify the number of digits used
title: The tableby function
text: Within tableby.control function 4 options controlling number significant digits shown. digits: controls number digits decimal place continuous values digits.count: controls number digits decimal point counts digits.pct: controls number digits decimal point percents digits.p: controls number digits decimal point p-values can specified per-variable basis using -formula functions specify tests run:
code: summary(tableby(arm ~ sex + age + fu.time, data=mockstudy), digits=4, digits.p=2, digits.pct=1) summary(tableby(arm ~ chisq(sex, digits.pct=1) + anova(age, digits=4) +                   anova(fu.time, digits = 1, digits.p = 6, format.p = FALSE), data=mockstudy))

title: The tableby function
text: purposes example, code creates trimmed mean function (trims 10%) use summarize data. Note use ... tells R pass extra arguments - required user-defined functions. case, na.rm=T passed myfunc. weights argument also required, even though isn’t passed internal function particular example. statistics formatted appropriately, may want use .tbstat(). example, suppose want create trimmed mean function trims 5 10 percent. first example shows separated comma; second puts 10% trimmed mean brackets. .tbstat() takes fmt= argument glue string specification, current value exposed x formatted value (using tbfmt()) exposed y. See ?.tbstat details. perhaps ’s useful put amount trimming parentheses. Since percent, can flag : Finally, ’s pre-computed summary statistic, can easily change formatting, like meanpmsd(): example:
code: trim10 <- function(x, weights=NULL, ...){   mean(x, trim=.1, ...) }  summary(tableby(sex ~ hgb, data=mockstudy,                  control=tableby.control(numeric.stats=c("Nmiss","trim10"), numeric.test="kwt",                     stats.labels=list(Nmiss='Missing values', trim10="Trimmed Mean, 10%")))) trim510comma <- function(x, ...){   tmp <- c(mean(x, trim = 0.05, ...), mean(x, trim = 0.1, ...))   as.tbstat(tmp, fmt = "{y[1]}, {y[2]}") } trim510bracket <- function(x, ...){   tmp <- c(mean(x, trim = 0.05, ...), mean(x, trim = 0.1, ...))   as.tbstat(tmp, fmt = "{y[1]} [{y[2]}]") }  summary(tableby(sex ~ hgb, data=mockstudy, numeric.stats=c("Nmiss", "trim510comma", "trim510bracket"), test = FALSE)) trim10pct <- function(x, ...){   tmp <- mean(x, trim = 0.05, ...)   as.tbstat(c(tmp, 10), fmt = "{y[1]} ({y[2]}%)", which.pct = 2L) } summary(tableby(sex ~ hgb, data=mockstudy, numeric.stats=c("Nmiss", "trim10pct"),                 digits = 2, digits.pct = 0, test = FALSE)) meanpmsd ## function (x, na.rm = TRUE, weights = NULL, ...)  ## { ##     if (na.rm && allNA(x)) { ##         as.tbstat(NA_real_) ##     } ##     else { ##         y <- meansd(x = x, na.rm = na.rm, weights = weights,  ##             ...) ##         attr(y, "fmt") <- "{y[1]} ± {y[2]}" ##         y ##     } ## } ## <bytecode: 0x16843c98> ## <environment: namespace:arsenal> dollarmean <- function(x, na.rm = TRUE, ...) {   if(na.rm && allNA(x)) {     as.tbstat(NA_real_)   } else {     out <- meansd(x, na.rm = na.rm, ...)     attr(out, "fmt") <- "${y[1]}"     out   } }  summary(tableby(sex ~ notest(age, "dollarmean", digits = 2), data = mockstudy)) ## what: 16. Use case-weights for creating summary statistics
title: The tableby function
text: comparing groups, often unbalanced comes nuisances age sex. tableby function allows create weighted summary statistics. option us used p-values calculated (test=FALSE). Case Weights used Case Weights used
code: ##create fake group that is not balanced by age/sex  set.seed(200) mockstudy$fake_arm <- ifelse(mockstudy$age>60 & mockstudy$sex=='Female',sample(c('A','B'),replace=T, prob=c(.2,.8)),                             sample(c('A','B'),replace=T, prob=c(.8,.4)))  mockstudy$agegp <- cut(mockstudy$age, breaks=c(18,50,60,70,90), right=FALSE)  ## create weights based on agegp and sex distribution tab1 <- with(mockstudy,table(agegp, sex)) tab2 <- with(mockstudy, table(agegp, sex, fake_arm)) tab2 ## , , fake_arm = A ##  ##          sex ## agegp     Male Female ##   [18,50)   73     62 ##   [50,60)  128     94 ##   [60,70)  139      7 ##   [70,90)  102      0 ##  ## , , fake_arm = B ##  ##          sex ## agegp     Male Female ##   [18,50)   79     48 ##   [50,60)  130     84 ##   [60,70)  156    166 ##   [70,90)  109    122 gpwts <- rep(tab1, length(unique(mockstudy$fake_arm)))/tab2 gpwts[gpwts>50] <- 30  ## apply weights to subjects index <- with(mockstudy, cbind(as.numeric(agegp), as.numeric(sex), as.numeric(as.factor(fake_arm))))  mockstudy$wts <- gpwts[index]  ## show weights by treatment arm group tapply(mockstudy$wts,mockstudy$fake_arm, summary) ## $A ##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  ##   1.774   1.894   2.069   2.276   2.082  24.714  ##  ## $B ##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  ##   1.000   1.042   1.924   1.677   1.985   2.292 orig <- tableby(fake_arm ~ age + sex + Surv(fu.time/365, fu.stat), data=mockstudy, test=FALSE) summary(orig, title='No Case Weights used') tab1 <- tableby(fake_arm ~ age + sex + Surv(fu.time/365, fu.stat), data=mockstudy, weights=wts) summary(tab1, title='Case Weights used')

what: 17. Create your own p-value and add it to the table
title: The tableby function
text: using weighted summary statistics, often desirable show p-value model corresponds weighted analysis. possible add p-value modify column title new p-value. Another use add standardized differences confidence intervals instead p-value. add p-value, simply need create data frame use function modpval.tableby(). first columns data.frame required: (1) -variable, (2) strata value (table strata term), (3) x-variable, (4) new p-value (test statistic). Another optional column can used indicate method used calculate p-value. specify use.pname=TRUE column name indicating p-value also used tableby summary. code: mypval <- data.frame(   byvar = "fake_arm",   variable = c('age','sex','Surv(fu.time/365, fu.stat)'),    adj.pvalue = c(.953,.811,.01),    method = c('Age/Sex adjusted model results') ) tab2 <- modpval.tableby(tab1, mypval, use.pname=TRUE) summary(tab2, title='Case Weights used, p-values added', pfootnote=TRUE)

title: The tableby function
text: default summary statistics, varible type, : numeric.stats: Continuous variables show default Nmiss, meansd, range cat.stats: Categorical factor variables show default Nmiss, countpct ordered.stats: Ordered factors show default Nmiss, countpct surv.stats: Survival variables show default Nmiss, Nevents, medsurv date.stats: Date variables show default Nmiss, median, range number extra functions defined specifically tableby function. N: count non-missing number observations particular group Npct: count non-missing number observations percentage column total (missing + non-missing) format N (%) Nrowpct: count non-missing number observations row-percentage (non-missings) format N (%) Nmiss: show count number missing values missing values Nmiss2: always show count number missing values variable within group Nmisspct: show count number missing values percentage column total (missing+non-missing) missing values Nmisspct2: Nmisspct, always show. meanse: print mean standard error format mean(se) meanCI: print mean (t) confidence interval count: print number values category countN: print number values category plus total N group format N/Total countpct: print number values category plus column-percentage format N (%) pct: print column-percentage countrowpct: print number values category plus row-percentage format N (%) rowpct: print row-percentage countcellpct: print number values category plus cell-percentage format N (%) binomCI: print proportion category plus binomial confidence interval. rowbinomCI: print row proportion category plus binomial confidence interval. medianq1q3: print median, 25th, 75th quantiles median (Q1, Q3) q1q3: print 25th 75th quantiles Q1, Q3 iqr: print inter-quartile range. medianrange: print median, minimum maximum values median (minimum, maximum) medianmad: print median median absolute deviation (mad) Nevents: print number events survival object within grouping level medSurv: print median survival NeventsSurv: print number events survival given times NriskSurv: print number still risk survival given times Nrisk: print number still risk given times medTime: print median follow-time sum max min mean sd var median range gmean, gsd, gmeansd, gmeanCI: geometric means, sds, confidence intervals.

title: The tableby function
text: tests used calculate p-values differ variable type, can specified explicitly formula statement control function. following tests accepted: anova: analysis variance test; default test continuous variables. grouping variable two levels, equivalent two-sample t-test equal variance. kwt: Kruskal-Wallis test, optional test continuous variables. grouping variable two levels, equivalent Wilcoxon Rank Sum test. wt: explicit Wilcoxcon test. medtest: Median test test, optional test continuous variables. chisq: chi-square goodness fit test Pearson chi-squared test; default categorical factor variables fe: Fisher’s exact test categorical variables; optional logrank: log-rank test, default test time--event variables trend: independence_test function coin used test trends. Whenthe grouping variable two levels, equivalent Armitage trend test. default ordered factors stddiff: perform standardized differences. notest: Don’t perform test.

title: Control settings for comparedf function — comparedf.control
text: following character strings accepted: tol.logical = "none": compare logicals exactly . tol.num = "absolute": compare absolute differences numerics. tol.num = "percent", tol.num = "pct" compare percent differences numerics. tol.char = "none": compare character strings exactly . tol.char = "trim": left-justify trim trailing white space. tol.char = "case": allow differences upper/lower case. tol.char = "": combine "trim" "case". tol.factor = "none": match character labels numeric levels. tol.factor = "levels": match numeric levels. tol.factor = "labels": match labels. tol.date = "absolute": compare absolute differences dates. tol.= "none": expect objects classes exactly identical. list names mapped x can used specify tolerances variable. One unnamed element supported default. tol.vars: set "none" (default) named vector,   tol.vars argument character vector denoting equivalence classes   characters variable names. single character vector means replace character   "". strings vector split character replaced first character string. E.g., character vector c("._", "aA", " ") denote dot underscore equivalent (translated dot),   "" "" equivalent (translated ""), spaces removed. special character string "case" vector specifying paste0(letters, LETTERS).

"e")    # remove all letter e's )  cntl <- comparedf.control(tol.char = list(   "none",      # the default   x1 = "case", # be case-insensitive for the variable "x1"   x2 = function(x, y) tol.NA(x, y, x != y | y == "NA") # a custom-defined tolerance ))

title: formulize — formulize
code: ## two-sided formula f1 <- formulize("y", c("x1", "x2", "x3"))  ## one-sided formula f2 <- formulize(x = c("x1", "x2", "x3"))  ## multi-sided formula f3 <- formulize("y", c("x1", "x2", "x3"), c("z1", "z2"), "w1")  ## can use numerics for column names data(mockstudy) f4 <- formulize(y = 1, x = 2:4, data = mockstudy)  ## mix and match f5 <- formulize(1, c("x1", "x2", "x3"), data = mockstudy)  ## get an interaction f6 <- formulize("y", c("x1*x2", "x3"))  ## get only interactions f7 <- formulize("y", c("x1", "x2", "x3"), collapse = "*")  ## no intercept f8 <- formulize("y", "x1 - 1") f9 <- formulize("y", c("x1", "x2", "-1"))  ## LHS as a list to use in arsenal functions f10 <- formulize(c("y1", "y2", "y3"), c("x", "z"), collapse.y = "list")  ## use in an lm f11 <- formulize(2, 3:4, data = mockstudy) summary(lm(f11, data = mockstudy)) #>  #> Call: #> lm(formula = f11, data = mockstudy) #>  #> Residuals: #>     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max  #> -41.800  -7.568   0.892   8.432  29.124  #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept)   60.1075     0.5966 100.744   <2e-16 *** #> armF: FOLFOX   0.6927     0.7088   0.977   0.3286     #> armG: IROX     0.1484     0.8118   0.183   0.8550     #> sexFemale     -1.2319     0.6105  -2.018   0.0438 *   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1 #>  #> Residual standard error: 11.51 on 1495 degrees of freedom #> Multiple R-squared:  0.003365,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.001365  #> F-statistic: 1.683 on 3 and 1495 DF,  p-value: 0.1688 #>   ## using non-syntactic names or calls (like reformulate example) f12 <- formulize(as.name("+-"), c("`P/E`", "`% Growth`")) f12 <- formulize("+-", c("P/E", "% Growth"), escape = TRUE)  f <- Surv(ft, case) ~ a + b f13 <- formulize(f[[2]], f[[3]])

title: Control settings for tableby function — tableby.control
text: test logical, telling tableby whether perform tests x variables across levels group variable. total logical, telling tableby whether calculate column totals across group variable. total.pos One "" "", denoting put total column relative -variable columns. test.pname character string denoting p-value column name summary.tableby. Modifiable also modpval.tableby. numeric.simplify, date.simplify logical, tell tableby whether condense numeric/date output single line. NOTE: simplifies one line one statistic reported, meansd. particular, Nmiss specified missings, output simplified. cat.simplify, ordered.simplify logical, tell tableby whether remove first level categorical/ordinal variable binary. TRUE, summary stats second level reported (unless one level, case reported). "label", second level's label additionally appended label. NOTE: simplifies one line one statistic reported, countpct. particular, Nmiss specified missings, output simplified. cat.droplevels levels dropped categorical variables? set true, p-values displayed unless test.always = TRUE well. numeric.test name test numeric RHS variables tableby: anova, kwt (Kruskal-Wallis), medtest (median test). LHS variable exists, mean required univariate test. cat.test name test categorical variables: chisq, fe (Fisher's Exact) ordered.test name test ordered variables: trend surv.test name test survival variables: logrank date.test name test date variables: kwt selectall.test name test date variables: notest test.always test performed even one -group 0 observations? Relevant kwt anova. numeric.stats, cat.stats, ordered.stats, surv.stats, date.stats, selectall.stats summary statistics include respective class RHS variables within levels group LHS variable. stats.labels named list labels statistics function names, function name named element list value goes string containing formal name printed printed renderings output, e.g., list(countpct="Count (Pct)"). unnamed elements ignored. Passing NULL disable labels. digits Number decimal places numeric values. digits.count Number decimal places count values. digits.pct Number decimal places percents. digits.p Number decimal places p-values. format.p Logical, denoting whether format p-values, character, glue specification format. See "Details", . digits.n Number decimal places N's header. Set NA suppress N's. conf.level Numeric, denoting confidence level use confidence intervals. (See, e.g., binomCI) wilcox.correct, wilcox.exact See wilcox.test chisq.correct logical, correction factor chisq.test simulate.p.value logical, simulate p-value categorical tests (fe chisq) B number simulations perform simulation-based p-value times vector times use survival summaries. ... additional arguments.

title: arsenal v3.7.0
text: first release several years. paired(): sign.test() now officially signtest(), former supported backwards compatibility. Added pct() rowpct(). (#337) Fixed one bug [.arsenal_table(). (#338) Allowed -level subsetting tableby() implementing [.tableby() method. (#336) tableby(): implemented stddiff(), computes standardized differences, instead p-values. Added stddiff “Suggests”. Remove formulize() default x=. Change logic escape=TRUE zero-length variables involved. Added meanpmsd() meanpmse(). (#343) Fixed issue long variable names deparse(). (#342) Missings non-missings: weights= longer allowed NAs Renamed Npct() Nrowpct() consistency. calculates number non-missing observations percentage row-total non-missing values. Removed na.rm= arguments N() Nrowpct() Added Nmisspct() Nmisspct2(). (#346) Changed label Nrowpct() Allowed stats= argument inline functions tableby() / paired(). (#348) Overhauled .tbstat() framework (#351): Edited summary statistic definitions borrow others Added print.tbstat() Removed .countpct(). Added tbfmt(). Added fmt= argument attribute, giving glue-specification format. Added glue imports. Custom-formatted p-values (#345): format.p= digits.p= now arguments inline functions. format.p= now accepts glue specifications.

Removed gam “Suggests”, favor splines, modelsum() vignette actually uses. Changed survival requirement >= 2.43-1. changes: Added code error informatively “Suggests” aren’t available. (#317) tableby(): Added selectall(). Added “label” option cat.simplify= ord.simplify= tableby()/paired(). (#288) Fixed bug tableby() / paired() stats.labels= specification remove default labels. (#316) Added wt() (Wilcoxon test) option tableby(). (#321) Fixed bug tableby() relating weird edge case -level “Total” total label set something else. Added feature tableby.control() allow total column moved columns. (#320) Added feature tableby.control() allow dropping categorical levels. (#318) Added meanse() tableby(). (#315) modelsum(): Added relrisk() modelsum() families (corresponding addition geepack package “Suggests”). (#279) Fixed bug modelsum() confidence level survival. Suppressed warnings new broom version using MASS::glm.nb(). comparedf(): Added option diffs() extracting -shared observations. (#305) Fixed bug comparedf() things infinite. (#306) write2(): Added note write2() vignette global option R Markdown documents R Studio. (#312)

(#296) Fixed bug conform new knitr publication, change knitr requirement >= 1.29. (#299, #300, #301) GitHub repository moved https://github.com/eheinzen/arsenal/ https://github.com/mayoverse/arsenal/. corresponding pkgdown site now https://mayoverse.github.io/arsenal/ freqlist(): Changed default addNA TRUE formula method, specify na.options like table method. modelsum(): Added support conditional logistic regressions. (#275) Fixed bug modelsum.control() confidence interval specification. Expanded statistic list survival models. Eliminated call broom::confint_tidy(). (#296) tableby() / paired(): Removed checks existance stat functions (check wasn’t working anyway) search enclosing environment well parent.frame() (custom stat functions). tableby(): better described .tbstat() .countpct() vignette, make .countpct() slightly flexible. (#283) paired(): fixed bug detecting stat functions. tableby(): added error reserved terms used -variable. (#277) Added option HTML footnotes (superscripts) summary.tableby(). (#298)

title: arsenal v3.4.0

(#167) tableby() / paired(): tableby(): Allowed changing “Overall” “Total” labels. (#253, #261) Allowed suppression N’s header. (#256, #36) Allowed digits formatting N’s header. Added support “min”, “max”, “sd”, “mean”, “var” (#259) “gmean”, “gsd”, “gmeansd”, “gmeanCI” (#260) “Npct” (#263) “sum” (#281). Added informative error message summary statistic computed. (#273) modelsum(): Fixed “statistic.F” family=“gaussian”. (#262) Fixed “Nevents” family=“survival”. (#266) Fixed vignette re: outputting CSV. title: arsenal v3.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2019-09-07 tableby() / paired(): Redid weights handled. user-visible changes standard deviations length-1 groups now reported NA instead NaN. Fixed bug modpval.tableby() factors involved. (#239) Added meanCI() medianmad(). (#230, #232) Added units difftime statistics using dates (e.g., meansd, medianmad, iqr). Fixed Chi-square Fisher’s Exact test one-level categorical variables. (#227, #228) Fixed n’s header using weights. (#229) Fixed bug confidence levels supplied control argument. (#234) paired(): fixed bug using count() factors. (#235) tableby.control(): added explicit times= argument survival summaries. Added option run statistical tests even one -group 0 observations. (#233, #250, #251) Stopped formatting p-values ’re numeric (, say, ’re pre-formatted user). (#249) modelsum(): Added functionality multiple adjustor sets. (#240) Fixed “Nmiss” “N” used strata, now report missings entire fit. (#241, #242, #243) Suppressed messages pROC::auc() calculating AUC. (#244) Fixed confidence level survival models. (#245) Added option likelihood ratio test main effect (adjustors): p.value.lrt (#238) Blanked p-values NA. (#246) code.chunk(): Fixed logic checking length chunk.opts=. Allowed empty code chunks. (#236) verbatim(): removed named argument favor dots; reworked object structure fix edge case printing oddities. (#248) Removed defunct functions.

title: arsenal v2.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2019-01-16 new class system ("arsenal_table") unifies tableby(), modelsum(), freqlist(). arsenal now imports re-exports utils::head() utils::tail(). arsenal now sticker! arsenal_table: Implemented new class (without constructor). labels<-.arsenal_table() doesn’t support unnamed labels, ’s unclear assign multiple -variables strata. also doesn’t give warnings labels used. Implemented sort.tableby(), errors object strata multiple -variables, runs default method. modpval.tableby() now requires first column -variable, object strata, second column required corresponding strata value. tests.tableby() now returns data.frame -variable column (applicable) strata column. na.tableby() now generates functions. “lhs=” argument determines whether remove NAs first column data. tableby() detects one-sided formula, sets FALSE. versions now remove rows NAs strata column (applicable). na.paired() now removes rows NAs strata column (applicable). padjust.tableby() padjust.summary.tableby() error fed object strata multiple -variables. .data.frame.tableby() .data.frame.summary.tableby() gain list.ok= argument, multiple left-hand-sides. Added logic statistical tests detect missing levels -variable. Fixed bug LaTeX formatting involving align= argument knitr::kable(). Passing term.name=TRUE summary.tableby() .data.frame.summary.tableby() now put term name top left corner table. modelsum(): Added functionality multiple -variables strata terms. required completely reworking innards modelsum object. na.modelsum() now removes rows NAs strata column (applicable). .data.frame.modelsum() .data.frame.summary.modelsum() gain list.ok= argument, multiple left-hand-sides. Passing term.name=TRUE summary.modelsum() .data.frame.summary.modelsum() now put term name top left corner table. freqlist(): Added functionality multiple -variables. required completely reworking innards freqlist object. Changed argument groupBy= strata= match tableby() modelsum(). Added merge.freqlist() .data.frame.summary.freqlist(). Note [.arsenal_table() now allows remove cumulative percent columns. Note labels<-.arsenal_table() longer supports unnamed labels, now accepts labels frequency, cumulative, percent columns freqlist objects. Removed digits=, sparse=, single=, dupLabels= arguments freqlist() summary.freqlist(). now arguments new freq.control(), passed dots (backwards compatibility). freqlist() also gained control= argument objects freq.control(). .data.frame.freqlist() longer rounds digits, label columns.
